coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode sensing system based on carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets with peroxidase-like activity for the detection of alkaline phosphatase and its inhibitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Sikai
Long, Xia
Wang, Lei
Lv, Lin
Li, Yue
Liu, Hongmei
He, Jingwen
Luo, Xiaojun
Zhang, Guoqi
Zhao, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is widely recognized as an important biomarker for clinical diagnosis. Herein, an innovative fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing method using MnO2 nanosheets and carbon dots (CDs) was developed to determine ALP activity. In the presence of H2O2, MnO2 nanosheets exhibited remarkable catalytic activity towards the oxidation reaction of catechol to produce o-benzoquinone. The generated o-benzoquinone effectively quenched the fluorescence of CDs through mechanisms of fluorescence resonance energy transfer and photo-induced electron transfer. ALP can catalyze the hydrolysis of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate to generate ascorbic acid, and the generated ascorbic acid can reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+, thereby inhibiting the oxidation of catechol to o-benzoquinone. This leads to both fluorescence recovery of CDs and a decrease in UV absorption. Using the developed fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing approaches, ALP activity was determined within the range of 0.5–100 U/L and 4–60 U/L, respectively, with corresponding detection limits of 0.13 and 1.18 U/L. Moreover, the proposed sensing approach was successfully applied to detect ALP in human serum samples and screen potential ALP inhibitors, demonstrating its applicability for medical diagnosis and pharmaceutical development applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
MnO2 nanosheets
Dual-mode detection
Alkaline phosphatase
Inhibitor screening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ALP detection in human serum samples
Characterization and properties of the CDs
Characterization and peroxidase-like properties of MnO2 nanosheets
Steady-state kinetic mechanism and catalytic mechanism of MnO2 nanosheets
Construction of dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric sensing platform using CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Analysis of ALP in real human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image; inset: HRTEM image, (B) the size distribution histogram, (C) XRD spectrum, and (D) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (E) UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectra of CDs. Inset: photograph of CDs under daylight (left) and under UV lamp at 365 nm (right). (F) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 380 to 470 nm.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of TMB, TMB/H2O2, MnO2/TMB, MnO2/TMB/H2O2, respectively. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of catechol, catechol /H2O2, MnO2/catechol, MnO2/catechol/H2O2, respectively. Insets were the corresponding photographs under daylight.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of H2O2, catechol, o-benzoquinone, AA2P, AA, and MnO2 nanosheets, and excitation-emission spectra of CDs. (B) The fluorescence decay curve of CDs in the absence and presence of o-benzoquinone. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra and (D) UV–Vis absorption of CDs system with different substances.

(A) Fluorescence and (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the sensing system with varying ALP concentrations (0–150 U/L). (B) The ratios of I/I 0 at 585 nm and (D) absorbance at 390 nm versus different ALP concentrations. Insets in (B) and (D) represent fitting curves of ALP concentrations versus the ratios of I/I 0 and absorbance, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the CDs and MnO2 nanosheets with peroxidase-like activity sensing platform for ALP activity monitoring and its inhibitor screening based on FRET and PET.
Detection of ALP in normal human serum samples.
Comparison of ALP detection in the serum of liver cancer patients by the proposed fluorometric method and the colorimetric method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: At a confidence level of 95% with four degrees of freedom, the tabulated value of t equals 2.776.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode sensing system based on carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets with peroxidase-like activity for the detection of alkaline phosphatase and its inhibitors

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Sikai Wang and Xia Long contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is widely recognized as an important biomarker for clinical diagnosis. Herein, an innovative fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing method using MnO2 nanosheets and carbon dots (CDs) was developed to determine ALP activity. In the presence of H2O2, MnO2 nanosheets exhibited remarkable catalytic activity towards the oxidation reaction of catechol to produce o-benzoquinone. The generated o-benzoquinone effectively quenched the fluorescence of CDs through mechanisms of fluorescence resonance energy transfer and photo-induced electron transfer. ALP can catalyze the hydrolysis of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate to generate ascorbic acid, and the generated ascorbic acid can reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+, thereby inhibiting the oxidation of catechol to o-benzoquinone. This leads to both fluorescence recovery of CDs and a decrease in UV absorption. Using the developed fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing approaches, ALP activity was determined within the range of 0.5–100 U/L and 4–60 U/L, respectively, with corresponding detection limits of 0.13 and 1.18 U/L. Moreover, the proposed sensing approach was successfully applied to detect ALP in human serum samples and screen potential ALP inhibitors, demonstrating its applicability for medical diagnosis and pharmaceutical development applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), an essential hydrolase present in numerous tissues of organisms such as placenta, liver, kidney, intestine, and bone, is able to catalyze the dephosphorylation of phosphorylated species including peptides, proteins, and nucleic acids, thereby playing an important role in regulating intracellular processes and signal transduction [1,2]. ALP is often employed as a crucial biomarker for disease diagnosis since abnormal levels of ALP are linked to many disorders, including lymphoma [3], liver dysfunction and cancer [4], breast cancer [5], prostatic cancer [6], diabetes [7], bone diseases [8], and hepatobiliary diseases [9]. Inhibiting ALP activity is beneficial in the treatment of these associated diseases. It is of great significance to discover potent and low-toxicity ALP inhibitors from natural products. Therefore, the construction of accurate and sensitive methods for analyzing ALP levels and screening potential inhibitors has garnered significant interest in clinical diagnosis and pharmaceutical development.

To date, numerous analytical techniques have been established for the detection of ALP, such as electrochemistry [10,11], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [12], chemiluminescence [13], phosphorescence [14,15], colorimetry [16,17], and fluorometry [18,19]. Among these analytical approaches, colorimetry and fluorometry are extensively employed due to their fast response, outstanding sensitivity, and simplicity [20]. However, the majority of the aforementioned approaches for determining ALP activity rely on a single signal mode, which is susceptible to interference, thereby compromising the accuracy of results. Dual-mode or multiple-mode approaches can effectively mitigate interference and increase the reliability of analytical findings through self-correcting and self-verifying [21]. Thus, there is a need for a sensing platform capable of outputting multiple signal modes for detecting ALP activity based on fluorescent materials with a facile and economical synthetic approach.

As a novel luminescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention due to their outstanding merits of remarkable luminescence, prominent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and facile chemical modification in comparison with semiconductor quantum dots, noble metal nanoparticles, and fluorescent dyes [22,23]. Recently, CDs have emerged as a highly promising candidate for the construction of fluorescent biosensors [24,25]. However, most of the obtained CDs emit blue or green fluorescence, which may be susceptible to interference from autofluorescence originating from the biological matrix, thus greatly limiting the further development of CDs in biological applications [26,27]. Therefore, the preparation of CDs with long-wavelength emission holds broad prospects in biosensing and bioimaging.

Nanozymes, a type of functional nanomaterials exhibiting enzyme-like catalytic activity, have emerged as promising candidates for natural enzymes [28]. Nanozymes offer numerous advantages over natural enzymes, including excellent catalytic activity, low deactivation, convenient preparation, low cost, high stability, and ease of modification [29,30]. A range of nanozymes, such as transition-metal-based nanozymes [31–35], transition-metal-oxide-based nanozymes [36–38], metal–organic framework-based nanozymes [39], and metal-free carbon nanozymes [40], have been developed for application in various fields, such as biomedicine, environmental science and technology, and analytical sensing [41–43]. Among them, MnO2 nanosheets have attracted considerable attention due to their good biocompatibility, large specific surface area, robust chemical stability, low cost, and peroxidase-like activity [44–46]. The extraordinary surface area of MnO2 nanosheets offers numerous catalytic active sites, thereby enhancing catalytic activity and potentially facilitating binding with copper-active species, which represents a crucial regulatory mechanism akin to that of natural enzymes. To the best of our knowledge, MnO2 nanosheets have been commonly used as fluorescent nanoquenchers for the fluorescence-based determination of ALP [47]. However, the peroxidase-like activity of MnO2 nanosheets can be utilized for catalyzing chromogenic substrates, resulting in the formation of colored products, thereby enabling the construction of colorimetric sensors. Thus, the combination of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets offers advantages in constructing a dual-mode sensing system.

Enlightened by the exceptional characteristics of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets, we developed a dual-mode sensing method for fluorometric and colorimetric determination of ALP utilizing CDs and MnO2 nanosheets. As depicted in Scheme 1 , H2O2 was catalyzed by MnO2 nanosheets to produce ·OH and ·O2 –, which can oxidize catechol to o-benzoquinone with an apparent absorption peak at 390 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of CDs at 585 nm was quenched by o-benzoquinone through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET). L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AA2P) can be hydrolyzed by ALP to produce ascorbic acid (AA) which could reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+ and block catechol oxidation to o-benzoquinone, thereby resulting in both restoration of CDs fluorescence and decreased UV absorption. When the ALP inhibitor appears, it leads to the inhibition of ALP activity, resulting in the loss of its ability to hydrolyze AA2P into AA. As a result, the absence of AA causes fluorescence quenching of CDs. Therefore, a dual-mode sensing platform for fluorometric and colorimetric determination of ALP activity and its inhibitors was successfully constructed.

Radix Scutellariae, Aloe vera, Cordyceps militaris, Galla Chinensis, and Murraya exotica were acquired from Chengdu branch of Beijing Tong Ren Tang (Chengdu, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), levodopa (L-Dopa), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS), catechol, magnesium chloride (MgCl2), N-ethylmaleimide (NEM), H2O2, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), AA, uric acid (UA), histidine (His), isopropanol (IPA), catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), L-terephthalic acid (TA), and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrolin-1-oxide (DMPO) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). ALP, AA2P, sodium orthovanadate (Na3VO4), p-nitrophenyl phosphate, lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), tyrosine (Tyr), L-cysteine (L-Cys), glutathione (GSH), glucose (Glu), horse radish peroxidase (HRP), hemoglobin (HB), acid phosphatase (ACP), lysozyme (LYZ), trypsin (TRY), xanthine oxidase (XOD), polyphenol oxidase (PPO), α-glucosidase (α-Glu), and thrombin (TB) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Serum samples of healthy people and patients with liver cancer were obtained from Zigong First People's Hospital. This research was reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of Zigong First People's Hospital (No. 2025–015).

UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra measurements were carried out on a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Japan). Fluorescence lifetimes were recorded with an Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were recorded using an MS-5000X spectrometer (Magnettech, Germany). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained on a Dimension FastScan atomic force microscope (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on an FEI Talos F200X instrument (FEI, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted on a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were collected using a Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

The CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal strategy [48]. Initially, 184 mg of L-Dopa and 108 mg of OPD were dissolved in deionized water (10.0 mL) by ultrasonication to form a transparent solution. Subsequently, the mixture was poured into a Teflon autoclave (50 mL) and maintained at 150 °C for 4 h. The CDs solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate insoluble impurities. The collected CDs solution was subjected to purification using a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted reagents. Finally, the dialysate was freeze-dried for subsequent applications.

The MnO2 nanosheets were prepared following a previously reported approach with some modifications [49]. Briefly, a mixture of 5 mL of SDBS (0.5 mol/L), 0.25 mL of H2SO4 solution, and 3.5 mL of ethanol was added to 20 mL of deionized water. After stirring for 15 min at 90°C, 2 mL of KMnO4 solution (0.05 mol/L) was slowly added dropwise into the above mixture and stirred at 90 °C for another 30 min. The color of the solution transitioned from purple to dark brown, confirming the preliminary formation of MnO2 nanosheets. The obtained MnO2 nanosheets were collected by centrifugation at 4200 rpm for 20 min and subsequently washed three times with deionized water. Finally, the MnO2 nanosheets were freeze-dried to obtain a dark brown powder.

For the ALP analysis, a mixture containing 100 μL of AA2P (25 mmol/L), 100 μL of MgCl2 (0.5 mmol/L) solution, 500 μL of Tris buffer (pH 8.0, 50 mmol/L), and 100 μL of ALP solution with different activities (0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, and 150 U/L) was incubated at 37 ℃ for 25 min. To the mixture, 200 μL of H2O2 (500 μmol/L), 100 μL of catechol (6 mmol/L), 200 μL of MnO2 nanosheets (0.5 mg/mL), and 450 μL of Tris buffer (pH 6.5, 50 mmol/L) were added, followed by incubation at 30 ℃ for 35 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of CDs solution (5 mg/mL) was added, and the mixture was incubated at 30 ℃ for 5 min. The resulting solution was then subjected to measurement of absorbance values at 390 nm and fluorescence intensity at 585 nm (excitation at 420 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP detection in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum samples were diluted 1:10 in PBS (50 mmol/L, pH 8.0) and treated with NEM (0.3 mmol/L, 100 μL) to eliminate reductive interferents (L-Cys and GSH) prior to the determination of ALP activity. The serum samples were subsequently spiked with ALP solutions at three varying concentrations (2, 5, and 10 U/L). Following that, both fluorometric and colorimetric assays were conducted as described in the above paragraph for determining ALP activity.

The applicability of the proposed screening strategy was validated by employing Na3VO4, a commonly employed ALP inhibitor. The process was conducted as follows: 100 μL of Na3VO4 solution at various concentrations (ranging from 5 μmol/L to 1000 μmol/L) was combined with 100 μL of MgCl2 (0.5 mmol/L), 500 μL of Tris buffer (pH 8.0, 50 mmol/L), and 100 μL of ALP (20 U/L). This mixture was then incubated at room temperature for 10 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of AA2P (25 mmol/L) was added, and the reaction was further incubated at 37 °C for 25 min. Sequential addition of H2O2 (500 μmol/L), catechol (6 mmol/L), MnO2 nanosheets (0.5 mg/mL), Tris buffer (pH 6.5, 50 mmol/L), and CDs solution (5 mg/mL) was performed for subsequent incubation steps. Lastly, the resulting mixtures were subjected to measurement of absorbance and fluorescence intensity.

In addition, the inhibitory potentials of Radix Scutellariae, Aloe vera, Cordyceps militaris, Galla Chinensis, and Murraya exotica were further explored employing the screening method. The process of extraction for medicinal plants involved the subsequent steps. One gram of dried powder from each medicinal plant was individually refluxed in 40 mL of 80% ethanol for 60 min. Subsequently, the resulting mixture underwent filtration and evaporation to yield a dry extract, which was ultimately diluted in PBS (50 mmol/L, pH 8.0) for the following inhibitory capacity experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and properties of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image demonstrated spherical morphology and high uniformity of the as-synthesized CDs (Fig. 1 A). The inset in Fig. 1A displays a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image, which reveals the excellent crystallinity of the CDs. The interlayer distance was measured to be 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the reflection of graphene planes. As illustrated in Fig. 1B, the size distribution of the CDs was relatively homogeneous, with dimensions ranging from 1.3 to 2.9 nm and an average diameter of approximately 2.14 nm. The XRD data (Fig. 1C) indicated that the as-synthesized CDs had a set of sharp diffraction peaks instead of the typical wide peaks observed at approximately 25°. Similar findings have also been reported in previous studies on synthesized CDs [50].

As depicted in Fig. 1D, the FT-IR spectra revealed the presence of surface groups on the CDs. The broad absorption band at 3471–3073 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds. The characteristic band at 1648 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of C=O bonds. The absorption bands at 1567 and 1418 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –COOH groups, respectively. Based on these FT-IR data, it can be inferred that the as-prepared CDs exhibit remarkable water solubility owing to the presence of hydrophilic surface groups such as carboxyl, hydroxy, and amino functionalities.

The XPS analysis was carried out to further study the chemical composition of CDs (Fig. S1). The full XPS spectra (Fig. S1A) presented three prominent peaks centered at 284.80, 400.38, and 531.64 eV, which were assigned to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum showed three distinct peaks representing C-C/C=C (284.7 eV), C-O (285.5 eV), and C=O/C=N (288.6 eV), respectively (Fig. S1B). Three binding energy peaks in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum at 398.7, 399.9, and 401.7 eV belonged to C=N, C-N-C, and N-H, respectively (Fig. S1C). Furthermore, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum presented two peaks at 531.8 and 532.6 eV, which corresponded to C=O and C-O-C, respectively (Fig. S1D). All the data obtained from XPS analysis were consistent with those acquired from FT-IR.

The spectral features of the CDs were confirmed by analyzing their fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra. The CDs displayed two distinct absorption peaks at 280 nm and 360 nm, which can be attributed to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and the n → π* transition of C=N/C=O, respectively (Fig. 1E). The optimal excitation wavelength was determined to be 420 nm, while the corresponding emission wavelength was found to be 585 nm (Fig. 1E). Additionally, under UV light irradiation at 365 nm, the light-yellow aqueous solution of CDs displayed orange-red fluorescence (inset in Fig. 1E). Furthermore, it was observed that the CDs exhibited excitation-independent emission under excitation wavelengths between 380 and 470 nm (Fig. 1F), indicating uniformity in particle size distribution and surface structure. In addition, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was determined to be 12.5% using an integrating sphere with excitation at 420 nm.

The stability assessment of the CDs in Fig. S2 demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained unchanged even after 180 min of exposure to 365 nm UV light (Fig. S2A), over a wide pH range spanning from 3 to 11 (Fig. S2B), and under different concentrations of NaCl ranging from 0 to 3 mol/L (Fig. S2C). These results verified the excellent stability exhibited by the as-prepared CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and peroxidase-like properties of MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. S3A, the MnO2 nanosheets exhibited a wrinkled two-dimensional structure. The AFM image and height profile demonstrated an approximate thickness of 3 nm for the MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. S3B and S3C), indicating their ultra-thin nature, which can provide an increased number of reaction sites on the substrate. Fig. S3D presented the XPS spectrum of the MnO2 nanosheets, where two peaks at 641.5 eV and 653.1 eV were observed and assigned to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 of MnO2, respectively [51]. Therefore, these results indicated the successful synthesis of MnO2 nanosheets.

The peroxidase-like behavior of MnO2 nanosheets was thoroughly investigated by employing TMB as the substrate for peroxidase activity. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, upon the introduction of H2O2 and MnO2 nanosheets, the colorless TMB solution transformed into a blue color (Inset of Fig. 2A), with a distinct absorption peak at 650 nm (line d in Fig. 2A). On the contrary, the reaction system remained nearly colorless in the absence of MnO2 nanosheets or H2O2 (lines b and c in Fig. 2A). Furthermore, catechol was employed as an additional peroxidase substrate to further validate the catalytic activity of MnO2 nanosheets. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, neither H2O2 alone (line b in Fig. 2B) nor MnO2 nanosheets alone (line c in Fig. 2B) efficiently oxidized catechol to produce a brown color. However, when both MnO2 nanosheets and H2O2 were added to the catechol solution, a brown color emerged (Inset of Fig. 2B), accompanied by a characteristic absorption peak observed at 390 nm (line d in Fig. 2B). These findings collectively verified the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of MnO2 nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic mechanism and catalytic mechanism of MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The steady-state kinetic parameters were determined to explore the kinetic mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of MnO2 nanosheets using H2O2 and TMB as substrates (Fig. S4). During the kinetic assays, one substrate concentration was varied while maintaining a constant level for the other substrate. The kinetic parameters, such as Michaelis−Menten constant (K m) and maximum velocity (V max), were calculated using the Lineweaver-Burk plot, 1/v = (K m/V max) × (1/[S]) + 1/V max, where K m stands for the Michaelis constant, v denotes the initial reaction rate, V max indicates the maximal reaction rate, and [S] indicates the substrate concentration of either H2O2 or TMB. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S4C and D, the double reciprocal curves exhibited a series of parallel lines, demonstrating the ping-pong mechanism [52]. MnO2 nanosheets exhibited lower K m values towards H2O2 and TMB substrates (0.0027 and 0.0208 mol/L, respectively) compared to HRP (Table S1), suggesting a higher affinity of MnO2 nanosheets towards these substrates.

The catalytic mechanism of MnO2 nanosheets was investigated by conducting experiments utilizing various scavengers targeting reactive oxygen species, including IPA (0.05, 0.1, 1 mmol/L), SOD (10, 50, 100 U/mL), CAT (10, 50, 100 U/mL), and His (0.05, 0.1, 1 mmol/L), which were employed to capture hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide anions (·O2 –), H2O2, and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively. As depicted in Fig. S5A, the absorbance of the system was unaffected by CAT and His. However, with increasing concentrations of IPA and SOD, there was a consistent decrease in absorbance at a wavelength of 650 nm. The obtained results indicated that the catalytic mechanism of MnO2 nanosheets lay in the generation of ·OH and ·O2 –. Moreover, TA was used to confirm the production of ·OH [53]. As depicted in Fig. S5B, non-fluorescent TA underwent oxidation by ·OH in the presence of H2O2, resulting in the formation of 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid with fluorescence. The fluorescent intensity exhibited a consistent augmentation with increasing concentration of MnO2 nanosheets, implying that MnO2 nanosheets could substantially expedite the decomposition of H2O2 into ·OH. Using DMPO as the spin-trapping agent, EPR results also directly corroborated the production of ·OH and ·O2 –. As shown in Fig. S5C and D, no discernible EPR signals were found in the mixture of DMPO with H2O2 or MnO2 nanosheets. Conversely, four equidistant signals were detected in the DMPO-H2O2-MnO2 system when evaluated in methanol and water, exhibiting an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 and 1:1:1:1, respectively. These findings indicated that the catalytic activity of MnO2 nanosheets could be ascribed to the production of ·OH and ·O2 –.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric sensing platform using CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: MnO2 nanosheets were capable of catalyzing the oxidation reaction of catechol to produce o-benzoquinone with the existence of H2O2. The generated o-benzoquinone was able to quench the fluorescence emission of CDs at 585 nm. The potential mechanism underlying the fluorescence quenching of CDs by o-benzoquinone was thoroughly explored. Notably, we observed a significant spectral overlap between the absorbance spectrum of o-benzoquinone and the emission spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3 A), suggesting a possible occurrence of FRET or inner-filter effect (IFE). As displayed in Fig. S6, the zeta potentials of CDs, o-benzoquinone, and CDs + o-benzoquinone complex are –42.95, +0.55, and –31.11 mV, respectively. These findings indicate a significant electrostatic interaction between CDs and o-benzoquinone, suggesting that the proximity between the two is sufficient to enable FRET. Additionally, Fig. 3B demonstrated that with the addition of o-benzoquinone, there was a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, indicating a FRET-based quenching mechanism [54]. Furthermore, as an electron acceptor, o-benzoquinone could cause the fluorescence quenching of CDs via PET [55,56].

The feasibility of the proposed method was further confirmed through fluorescence and UV–vis spectra analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 3C and 3D, the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the CDs solution did not show any significant alterations upon individual addition of H2O2, catechol, MnO2, or H2O2/MnO2. However, when MnO2, H2O2, and catechol coexisted, o-benzoquinone was generated, leading to quenching of CDs fluorescence at 585 nm and an increase in UV absorption at 390 nm. Subsequent addition of AA2P did not induce any changes in the fluorescence intensity or absorbance of the system solution. In the presence of CDs, ALP, AA2P, MnO2 nanosheets, H2O2, and catechol together, ALP catalyzed the generation of AA from AA2P, which exhibited strong reducing properties, effectively eliminating both H2O2 and ·OH from the solution. Meanwhile, MnO2 nanosheets were reduced to Mn2+ (Fig. S7), causing a loss of their peroxidase-like activity. As shown in Fig. S8, the production of Mn2+ was additionally confirmed through its highly specific interaction with IO4 −. Consequently, o-benzoquinone formation was hindered, leading to the fluorescence recovery of CDs as well as a decrease in UV absorption. Thus, a highly sensitive fluorometric and colorimetric detection strategy for ALP was successfully constructed.

In addition, various experimental factors were optimized for the construction of CDs/MnO2/H2O2/catechol sensing system, including CDs concentration, catechol concentration, reaction pH (Tris buffer), reaction temperature, and reaction time. As shown in Fig. S9, to achieve the minimum ratio of I/I 0 (where I 0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 585 nm before and after the addition of the substance), the optimal conditions were determined as follows: a CDs concentration of 2 mg/mL, a catechol concentration of 6 mmol/L, a reaction pH of 6.5, a reaction temperature of 30 ℃, and a reaction time of 35 min.

The pH (Tris buffer), enzyme incubation temperature, and enzyme incubation time were systematically studied to develop a highly sensitive sensing system for ALP detection. The maximum I/I 0 ratio was achieved at pH 8.0, which was subsequently chosen for further investigation (Fig. S10A). The results of the enzyme incubation temperature study revealed that the highest I/I 0 ratio was observed at 37 ℃, which closely resembled human body temperature and thus was chosen as the optimal enzyme incubation temperature (Fig. S10B). As shown in Fig. S10C, the I/I 0 ratio exhibited a gradual increase and reached a plateau after 25 min. Thus, 25 min was chosen as the optimal enzyme incubation time.

The analytical capabilities of the fluorometric and colorimetric sensing system for ALP determination were evaluated under the optimal experimental conditions (Fig. 4 ). As depicted in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 585 nm rose progressively with the increasing concentration of ALP. A strong linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9949) was found between the I/I 0 ratio and ALP concentration in the range of 0.5–100 U/L (inset in Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.13 U/L, calculated as 3 times the standard deviation-to-slope (3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of eleven blank signals, and K denotes the slope of the calibration curve). Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 4C, a significant decrease in system absorbance was observed with increasing concentrations of ALP. The calibration curve exhibited a robust linear correlation between absorbance at 390 nm and ALP activity in the range of 4 to 60 U/L with R 2 = 0.9936 (inset in Fig. 4D). Additionally, the LOD was determined to be 1.18 U/L.

To assess the selectivity and anti-interference capability of this developed sensing system, we investigated the impact of possible interfering ingredients such as metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+), amino acids (Gly, Tyr, Lys, L-Cys), and biomolecules (UA, Glu, HB, ACP, LYZ, TRY, α-Glu, PPO, XOD, TB, GSH) on both fluorescence intensity and absorbance. As illustrated in Fig. S11, with the exception of mercapto molecules (L-Cys and GSH), no significant interference was observed in the fluorescence intensity and absorbance from other substances. Nevertheless, the interference caused by L-Cys and GSH could be effectively eliminated using a unique thiol-masking agent called NEM. Upon incubation of L-Cys or GSH with NEM, there were negligible changes in fluorescence intensity and absorbance. These findings suggested that the sensing system based on CDs and MnO2 nanosheets exhibited excellent selectivity and anti-interference capacity for ALP detection due to the accurate specificity of ALP for its substrate, along with the preprocessing of samples.

The overexpression of ALP has been implicated in the pathogenesis of some diseases, including liver and skeletal disorders. Therefore, inhibiting the enzymatic activity of ALP holds promise for therapeutic interventions against these related diseases. Na3VO4, an acknowledged ALP inhibitor, was selected as an example to evaluate the performance of the fluorometric and colorimetric sensing system for screening ALP inhibitors. As illustrated in Fig. S12A and S12C, a noticeable decrease in fluorescence intensity accompanied by a rise in absorbance was observed as the concentration of Na3VO4 increased within the range of 0–1000 μmol/L. By employing fluorometric and colorimetric methods, classical sigmoidal curves were generated by plotting the logarithm of Na3VO4 concentration against fluorescence or absorption signals (Fig. S12B and S12D). The half-maximal inhibitory concentrations (IC50) of Na3VO4 were determined to be 50.12 and 120.23 μmol/L using the fluorometric and colorimetric methods, respectively. The results demonstrated that the developed sensing system exhibited promising potential for the screening of ALP inhibitors.

In addition, there has been growing interest in the development of secure and potent inhibitors derived from natural plants for medical applications. As shown in Fig. S13A, the IC50 values of ethanol extracts obtained from Radix Scutellariae, Aloe vera, Cordyceps militaris, Galla Chinensis, and Murraya exotica were 24.55, 16.35, 28.20, 10.34, and 15.78 mg/mL, respectively, as determined by the colorimetric method. As shown in Fig. S13B, the IC50 values of ethanol extracts obtained from Radix Scutellariae, Aloe vera, Cordyceps militaris, Galla Chinensis, and Murraya exotica were 56.17, 35.29, 59.15, 22.15, and 33.61 mg/mL, respectively, as determined by the colorimetric method. Therefore, this developed method holds great promise for screening ALP inhibitors from natural plants to facilitate new drug development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ALP in real human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicality of the developed fluorometric and colorimetric methods, the determination of ALP levels was performed in actual samples. As indicated in Table 1 , the average recoveries detected by the fluorometric assay varied from 94.0% to 106.5%, exhibiting relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 4.4% (intra-day, n = 3) and 5.4% (inter-day, n = 9), respectively. Comparably, employing the colorimetric assay yielded average recoveries ranging from 90.5% to 105.5%, with intra-day RSDs (n = 3) and inter-day RSDs (n = 9) both lower than 4.2%. To assess the reproducibility of the biosensor, the recoveries of ALP (spiked concentration = 5 U/L) were compared under identical conditions using different batches of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets (n = 6). The obtained RSDs for CDs and MnO2 nanosheets from various batches in both fluorometric and colorimetric assays for determining ALP activity were found to be 5.6% and 6.8%, respectively, suggesting a high level of reproducibility in the preparation process. As shown in Table S2, the developed approach exhibited a broader linear range and a lower LOD in comparison to earlier approaches for ALP detection, suggesting its excellent sensitivity and enhanced analytical performance. Besides, the newly prepared carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial not only exhibits a more favorable fluorescence emission peak (585 nm) compared to other reported fluorescent nanomaterials, but also avoids issues such as poor photostability, environmental toxicity, or high cost associated with traditional fluorescent dyes, noble metal nanoclusters, and rare earth luminescent materials, thereby enhancing its suitability for biosensing and imaging applications. Of even greater significance, the dual-mode method is able to improve the reliability of analysis outcomes through self-correcting and self-verifying mechanisms.

Moreover, a t-test was conducted to compare the ALP levels in serum samples from liver cancer patients as detected by the developed fluorometric and colorimetric assays. As shown in Table 2 , with a 95% confidence level, all measured t-values were lower than the critical value of 2.776, suggesting no obvious distinction between data acquired from both detection modes. Additionally, it was observed that serum samples from patients with liver cancer exhibited considerably greater levels of ALP activity (Table 2) compared to those collected from healthy individuals (Table 1), indicating that ALP activity could be a valuable clinical indicator for disease diagnosis. Furthermore, as illustrated in Table S3 and S4, the findings derived from the proposed colorimetric and fluorometric methods demonstrated a high degree of consistency with those derived from the classical p-nitrophenyl phosphate-based colorimetric approach. The results indicated the applicability of our sensing system for determining ALP activity in intricate biological matrices.

In conclusion, an innovative fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing system for ALP assay and inhibitor screening was developed for the first time based on CDs and MnO2 nanosheets with peroxidase-like activity. The results demonstrated excellent sensitivity and selectivity for the fluorometric and colorimetric assays in determining ALP activity, with LODs of 0.13 and 1.18 U/L, respectively. Furthermore, the feasibility of this sensing strategy was validated by successfully measuring ALP activity in actual human serum samples and screening for potential ALP inhibitors. This study not only offers an effective way for ALP-related determination utilizing new materials and techniques, but also holds considerable promise in clinical diagnosis and medication research.

Sikai Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xia Long: Resources. Lei Wang: Validation, Methodology. Lin Lv: Validation, Methodology. Yue Li: Methodology, Investigation. Hongmei Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Jingwen He: Validation, Methodology. Xiaojun Luo: Resources, Conceptualization. Guoqi Zhang: Resources, Conceptualization. Yan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by West Light Foundation of the Chinese academy of sciences (to YZ), and the Science and Technology Programs of Sichuan Province of China (No. 2024NSFSC2077).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113624.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Yang, FRET effect between fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles and MnO2 nanosheets and its application for sensitive sensing of alkaline phosphatase, ACS Appl. Mater.
